meta.title: 12.2 Authorization Patterns
meta.description: RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, and policy engines for access control in distributed systems.

blocks.prompt: Your SaaS platform has admins, editors, and viewers. An editor should be able to edit only documents in their own team's workspace — not other teams' documents. Simple role checks (isEditor) are not enough. What do you need?

blocks.content:
## Authorization models

Authentication answers "who are you?" Authorization answers "what can you do?" In distributed systems, authorization must be fast, consistent, and expressive enough to model real-world access rules.

### Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

Assign permissions to roles, assign roles to users.

```
Roles: admin, editor, viewer
Permissions: admin → [read, write, delete, manage_users]
             editor → [read, write]
             viewer → [read]
User Alice → role: editor → can read and write
```

**Strengths:** Simple to understand, easy to audit, works well for coarse-grained access. **Weaknesses:** Role explosion — when you need editor-of-project-X, editor-of-project-Y, you create hundreds of roles. Cannot express contextual rules (time of day, IP address, resource ownership).

### Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

Decisions based on attributes of the user, resource, action, and environment.

```
Policy: ALLOW if user.department == resource.department
              AND action == "read"
              AND environment.time in business_hours
```

**Strengths:** Extremely flexible. No role explosion. Can express any policy. **Weaknesses:** Hard to audit ("who can access what?" requires evaluating all attribute combinations). Policies can become complex and difficult to debug.

### Relationship-Based Access Control (ReBAC)

Access based on the relationship graph between users and resources. Made popular by Google's Zanzibar paper.

```
Document:budget#owner@User:alice
Folder:finance#viewer@Team:accounting
Document:budget#parent@Folder:finance
→ Members of Team:accounting can view Document:budget (inherited through folder)
```

**Strengths:** Natural for hierarchical and collaborative systems (Google Drive, GitHub). Handles ownership, sharing, and inheritance elegantly. **Weaknesses:** Requires a relationship graph store. Latency of graph traversal.

### Comparison

| Model | Best for | Complexity | Flexibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| RBAC | Simple apps, coarse permissions | Low | Low |
| ABAC | Context-dependent rules, compliance | High | Very high |
| ReBAC | Collaborative apps, resource hierarchies | Medium | High |
| Hybrid (RBAC + ABAC) | Most production systems | Medium | High |

### Policy engines

Externalize authorization logic from application code into a dedicated policy engine.

- **Open Policy Agent (OPA):** General-purpose. Policies written in Rego. Used for API authorization, Kubernetes admission control, and infrastructure policies.
- **Cedar (AWS):** Purpose-built for application authorization. Used by AWS Verified Permissions.
- **SpiceDB / Authzed:** Open-source ReBAC engine inspired by Google Zanzibar.

**Why externalize?** Consistent enforcement across services. Policy changes without code deploys. Auditable. Testable. Separates business logic from access logic.

blocks.question: You are building a healthcare platform. Doctors should see only their own patients' records. Nurses on a specific ward should see records of patients currently on that ward. Admins can see everything. A doctor covering for another doctor should temporarily see the other doctor's patients. Which authorization model(s) would you use and why?
blocks.answer:
**Hybrid ReBAC + ABAC approach.** ReBAC models the core relationships: Doctor→Patient (treating relationship), Nurse→Ward→Patient (ward assignment), Admin→Organization (global access). The temporary coverage scenario is modeled as a time-bounded relationship: Doctor:Bob#covering@Doctor:Alice with a start/end time attribute (ABAC element).

**Implementation:** Use a ReBAC engine (SpiceDB) for relationship-based checks. Add ABAC-style attributes for time-bounded and contextual rules. A policy engine (OPA or Cedar) evaluates composite policies that combine relationship checks with attribute conditions.

**Why not pure RBAC?** Role explosion — you would need a role per doctor-patient pair. **Why not pure ABAC?** The relationship model (doctor treats patient, patient is on ward) is naturally a graph, not a set of attribute comparisons. **ReBAC alone** does not handle the time-bounded coverage scenario cleanly without attribute support.

blocks.content: RBAC is simple but limited. ABAC is flexible but hard to audit. ReBAC naturally models ownership and hierarchies. Most production systems use a hybrid approach with an externalized policy engine (OPA, Cedar, SpiceDB) for consistent, auditable, and testable authorization.

blocks.prompt: Module 12.3 covers encryption and data protection — securing the data that authorized users access.
